nativeRange.habitat: Coral and rocky reefs, surge zones; typically found at depths of 10–61 m

diet.notes: Primarily grazes on macroalgae and microalgae. Has been documented accepting nori, spirulina, zucchini, and broccoli in aquariums. Occasional meaty foods such as brine shrimp or mysis shrimp have been reported to provide nutritional balance. Generally advised to offer algae-based foods at least three times per week.

summary: A rare and prized surgeonfish from the Western Indian Ocean, generally considered reef-safe and herbivorous; tends to be territorial toward conspecifics and other Zebrasoma tangs in captivity.
careNotes:
The Gemmatum Tang (also sold as 'Gem Tang') is one of the rarer and more expensive members of the Zebrasoma genus, frequently collected around Mauritius and the surge zones of the southwestern Indian Ocean. It has been reported to be an active and curious swimmer that grazes throughout the day on live rock and algae.

In the wild, adults are generally described as extremely territorial and solitary. In aquariums, specimens tend to be semi-aggressive, particularly toward other tangs — especially other Zebrasoma species and fish with a similar flattened, disc-like body shape. Juveniles are generally less colorful than adults; the characteristic white spots intensify with maturity.

As a surgeonfish, it possesses sharp caudal-peduncle scalpels on either side of the tail base; these should be handled with caution during net transfers.

Like most tangs, a diet rich in marine algae has been reported to support immune health and reduce aggression. A varied herbivore diet supplemented occasionally with small meaty foods is generally advised.

Lifespan reports vary considerably across sources: some hobbyist sources cite approximately 10 years in well-maintained aquariums, while others report up to 30 years — a spread consistent with the broader Acanthuridae family but uncertain for this specific species. See dataStatus note.

Temperature sourcing note: LiveAquaria lists 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C); FishBase reports 24–28°C; a third hobbyist source lists 74–82°F (23.3–27.8°C). Averaged across all three sources: min ≈ 23°C, max ≈ 27°C.

Salinity note: LiveAquaria lists SG 1.020–1.025; hobbyist care guides list 1.024–1.026. Averaged: min 1.022, max 1.026.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity has not been documented in the available sources. Like most Acanthuridae, captive reproduction is generally considered extremely difficult.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (22 cm confirmed), native range, temperature range (24–28°C), taxonomy (Acanthuridae / Acanthuriformes), and habitat notes ('extremely territorial and solitary').

additional.name: Fish Laboratory
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (22 cm), minimum tank size (180 gal), reef-safe status, diet type, and lifespan (reported 30 years — notably higher than other sources).

additional.name: Saltwater Aquarium Blog
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (22 cm), minimum tank size (180 gal / 681 L), care level (moderate), lifespan (reported ~10 years — considerably lower than Fish Laboratory's 30-year claim, triggering needs_review on lifespanYears).

fish.breedingNotes: No documented captive breeding. Acanthuridae larvae have a pelagic phase that makes captive rearing extremely difficult; reproduction has not been reported for this species in aquarium settings.
